text: The surgeon begins the robotic tap meshplasty procedure for bilateral inguinal hernia by placing ports and making a peritoneal incision approximately two centimeters from the horizontal line at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).

The surgeon enters the extraperitoneal or preperitoneal space, encountering loose areolar tissue. Cold scissors and short bursts of energy are used to create a wide dissection plane, avoiding major vessels.

The surgeon performs systematic dissection in zone one, known as the triangle of pain, using robotic instruments. The dissection is carried out horizontally to provide ample space for the robotic tools, avoiding direct visualization of nerves to prevent damage.

The surgeon enters the medial compartment, zone two, and encounters the rectal branch of the inferior epigastric artery, indicating correct dissection. The dissection progresses towards the lighthouse, a bony landmark, while additional clearance is performed along the lateral pelvic wall. Inferiorly, the dissection extends approximately three centimeters below the pubic arch to establish an adequate plane.

The surgeon mobilizes the superior leaf of the peritoneum, taking care to avoid injury to the inferior epigastric vessels to prevent bleeding. The indirect inguinal hernia sac is visible, characterized by a short mouth and a long seed-like appearance, as it is dissected from surrounding tissues within the triangle.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the gonadal vessels and the vas deferens, ensuring their protection while dissecting below the hernial sac. The dissection forms a tunnel beneath the sac, with the iliac vessels located deeper within the triangle of doom.

The surgeon seeks additional clearance on the medial side to identify and preserve the vas deferens. In younger patients and those with long-standing hernias, the lateral pelvic wall and tissues at the triangle of doom present tougher dissection challenges.

The surgeon widens the previously formed tunnel beneath the hernial sac, ensuring the vas deferens is safeguarded. The indirect hernia sac is divided near its apex, avoiding entry into the scrotum and preserving the testicular vessels and surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection and uses energy devices to carefully divide the hernia sac, ensuring the vas deferens is not involved. The technique involves holding and releasing the tissue to utilize its elasticity for identification and dissection.

The surgeon identifies a white structure below the hernia sac, with a layer of tissue on top, indicating the preservation of the vas and its vasculature. Further dissection is performed on the lateral pelvic wall to ensure adequate division of tissues, preventing mesh folding or buckling. A flat, two-dimensional mesh measuring twelve by fifteen centimeters is placed into the extraperitoneal pouch. Articulating instruments are used to maneuver the mesh into its perfect position, followed by additional dissection to prevent folding and displacement upon deflation of the pneumoperitoneum.

So I use three-dimensional meshes in few patients and two-dimensional meshes in the other ones. There are some clinical criteria that I follow and then there are some anatomical criteria that I follow but this that can be discussed in a separate video.
The surgeon performs additional dissection in the superomedial region to ensure the mesh is properly positioned and does not fold at the top, allowing it to sit comfortably in place.

The surgeon performs a camera flip maneuver, adjusting the angle from thirty degrees up to thirty degrees down using an angled camera. The mesh is observed to be adequately positioned, and the surgeon proceeds with dissection on the opposite side, following the same steps of dissection, mesh placement, fixation, and closure of the peritoneum.

The surgeon adjusts the camera angle and proximity to obtain a closer view of critical tissues. They create the extraperitoneal space, carefully navigating within the correct plane to avoid encountering vessels and nerves, thereby preventing injuries.

The surgeon views the surgical field in three dimensions from the console, enhancing depth perception. The inferior epigastric vessels are prominently visible on the screen. Dissection continues in the medial compartment, referred to as zone two, with the left-hand side landmark visible.

The surgeon carefully bisects the area near the apparent obturator vessels, exercising caution to safeguard these structures. Dissection continues at the medial border of the triangle of doom, using gentle push and pull techniques to develop the plane with minimal use of cautery. The loose areolar tissue is visible, and the surgeon takes care to prevent bleeding that could obscure the view and disrupt the plane.

The surgeon dissects the fat of the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring it remains on the bladder. This technique aids in firm integration of the mesh on the abdominal wall and prevents the bladder from becoming involved in the fibrotic process.

The surgeon places the mesh on the left side for reinforcement, ensuring it sits flat within the pouch. Additional dissection is performed to facilitate proper mesh placement, creating a large common space for the connected meshes.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh is positioned with adequate space behind the pubic bone and creates additional lateral space to prevent any folding of the mesh, resulting in a flat placement. The procedure then transitions to the suturing phase.

The surgeon uses a Vicryl 3-0 suture to place interrupted sutures for mesh fixation, adjusting based on the hernia type and size. The sutures are not overly tight, allowing for air knotting. The surgeon approaches the challenging task of suturing and fixing the mesh onto the bone.

The surgeon uses a backhand suturing technique to perform medial fixation superiorly, following previous lateral and medial fixation steps. The sutures are applied using a fine three-zero suture, which is visually small and delicate.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum on the right side of the screen using Vicryl 3-0 sutures in a running fashion, completing the procedure.

The surgeon uses Vicryl 3-0 sutures to achieve peritoneal apposition, applying running sutures to complete the closure of the middle end of the peritoneal incision. The sutures are interlocked to ensure tightness and prevent loosening.

The surgeon uses a barbed suture to secure the medial end, ensuring it does not slip easily. They proceed to close the indirect herniated opening, specifically the deep ring and peritoneum. After completing the right side, the surgeon begins inferior fixation on the left side, adapting the approach based on anatomical convenience.

The surgeon performs additional fixation of the mesh to the bone, taking care to secure the periosteum with shallow needle bites to ensure the mesh remains immobile. Sutures on the soft tissue are left slightly loose to allow for micro-movement, promoting a pliable mesh fibrosis complex.

The surgeon forms a loop or lasso with the suture, starting from the lateral angle and progressing to the medial angle. The peritoneum at the medial angle is manipulated with extra push-pull movements, allowing for greater flexibility compared to the lateral angle.

The surgeon repeats the closure step on the opposite side, removing laxity and securing the closure with interlocking sutures. Additional running sutures are placed to finalize the peritoneal closure.
